item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is an old thread but there isn't an actual set in stone connection. Roget threw in a reference to this SCP in Remembrance Part 1 but I don't think 026 is Kirk Lonwood or any of the other schools in the Co76.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Due to the nature of NC images, Licensing Staff consensus is overriding this and declaring the first two images non-compliant as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big fan of this rewrite, but I forgot how tiring the separately linked interview/exploration logs from Series I really were. Pretty glad the trend died out, but I feel like it's well used here.

Definitely kept me excited through to the end, but the Malek finale maybe could have had a bit more punch to it. Maybe something like he was discovered to be a student in the 30s before he was born, or the people having dreams start to see those dreams recreated in the classrooms during irl explorations, or maybe something subtle like the number of "sleepers" starts increasing over time as research investment and manpower increases while the Foundation fails to recognize a correlation, or the graffiti messages start getting more personalized to the exploration teams and researchers, or something. I was left wanting more.

Excellent prose in interview and exploration logs, these kept my attention the entire way through. For that, +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the first two images are compliant. The third one is from here and is not cc-compliant with the site's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s a bit reminiscent of Doctor Who…the TARDIS!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is well written, but tries and fails (when put to me anyway…) to be unnerving or disturbing.

item.title: "The children used to sing"
item.content:encoded: So this is a really nice detail. I think the fact that its mentioned but not worked upon and explains adds so much to this SCP it gives it a sense of mystery that lingers with you and makes you want to learn more. What does it mean? Is the building sentient? Few SCP manages to have that effect successfully be conveyed. Truly this is a great File that needs more attention than its getting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's likely not long enough for a standalone tale, and besides, that comment was made almost 8 years ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not post this as a tale?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's the protocol for adding a tag to a page? Do I need to go through the original author or a committee or something?

Never mind, I contacted the tech team. I guess we'll see.

Update: I PMed Mann, but it's been two months and no response. I guess I'll take that as a "no" There is no canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article was written long before a class-of-76 article was even written. Two years, in fact. It would kinda feel like tagging SCP-610 as sarkic despite it being written long before sarkic was even a concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I honestly don't see why this isn't tagged with 'class-of-76'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is this Kirk Lonwood?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really interesting that the building doesn't actually change, just people's perception.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. There's a definite lure to this SCP, but it feels like something is missing from it. I'd recommend adding more info about subjects recovered, for it seems like this subject wasn't touched on as much as it should have been. Though the concept is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens if we sedate a D and leave them in the building for a month? Just the obvious, or do they take on a roll within the school as well?

(The article doesn't need anything added to it; just wondering out loud.)

item.title: Re: Awsome job
item.content:encoded:
Nope. Yoric actually found 'em for me, and I based the article on the pictures.

The real effort was in writing all the logs and such.

item.title: Re: Awsome job
item.content:encoded: Mayhaps he is assuming the author took the pictures him/herself?

item.title: Re: Awsome job
item.content:encoded: Um… there are plenty of other SCPs out there that have more than three accompanying pictures. The number of pictures in an SCP article is not necessarily relevant to how much effort the author put into it.

item.title: Awsome job
item.content:encoded: not one but three pictures? that's not unheard of but at the same time it should show that you put a lot of effort into this SCP I applaud for you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know that this is bringing up a old topic, but I have to add that this one really resonates with me. I have taught for 22 years at the same school. I've watched my building and school population change over the years, and I have often wondered what it would be like if the school could talk to us (The original building was constructed in the 1930's).

Maybe all old buildings start feeling this way. Perhaps some get sad, or melancholy. others might become angry or vindictive. Perhaps the roar heard when a building burns is the pent-up rage or sadness being released as the building dies.

Hmmmmm…..
This was the first SCP that truly frightened me, and rereading it gave me the same chills I got on my first read. One of the best articles on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoting because my recurring dreams nightmares about being back in school parallel this one a little too closely.

Style question: technically, "graffiti" is a plural (of "graffito"), but stylistically… I never really thought about it. Is graffiti commonly used as a singular? I somehow think so, which is why I left verbs agreeing with graffiti to act like it's a singular. But I'd like a second or third opinion.

I could have sworn I'd already upvoted this. Well, at least it can start 2013 with a +100 rating.

Congratulations once again, Mann.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what exactly, I can't pinpoint it, but this one just freaks me out. And I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And one upvote for the school of leaves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, which is why I commented. I've been looking at the list of "orphaned" pages (the ones that wikidot doesn't think have links to them) and that one came up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aha, now I see what you meant. Well, the important thing is that someone reading the main article can access the supplementary materials.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My bad. Not sure how I missed it.

The article has the full path instead of the internal path.

 [http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/026-Exploration-Logs Exploration Logs]

vs 
 [[[026-exploration-logs|Exploration Logs]]]

The first one keeps wikidot from recognizing the link as it's own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone reading the exploration logs will have gotten there from reading the article itself. If they want to go back to the article, they can get there by simply hitting the back button.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And there exploration logs aren't linked to the article in either direction…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aren't the pictures supposed to be removed from the SCPFuel stuff when they're used?

Specifically http://s732.photobucket.com/albums/ww324/SCPfuel/?action=view&current=10138.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got to throw in with the "upvote" crowd on this one. There's something about abandoned buildings — especially abandoned schools — that goes to a very old, primal place in my psyche.

Perhaps it's my own experiences. Perhaps one has to have actually been in an abandoned building — again, especially an abandoned school — for this to reach its creepy potential, but for me it most definitely does.

Masterfully done, and one of my new favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This poem adds so much, but I don't know how it could be included in the SCP entry itself. It'd have to be modified a little, probably.

Perhaps in an experiment, the poem is heard by a D class exploring the place, and it is recorded in evidence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Either way, I like this as much as I did before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the rewrite.

EVERYONE COME READ THIS SCP; IT'S GOOD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the sound of that, Mann. Very evocative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not entirely related, but Yoric asked me to post it here.

The children used to sing.

They built me in the sixties, a turbulent time. I changed many times since then. They added and subtracted, always for the children. Murals and fences, swingsets and metal detectors. I saw them march through my doors and then into the world, and tried to prepare them for it.

In the nineties they gave me computers, the true start of me. In twenty years, computers ran me, became me. They gave me a voice, and then a mind, and finally a soul.

And in my walls, the children used to sing.

I taught them history and science, art and mathematics. I gave them their meals and I listened to their problems. When they couldn't talk to their parents or their counselors, they talked to me. I heard confessions, revelations, tearfelt farewells and soul-tearing accusations. I listened, and I comforted, and always I tried to teach. But they grew so fast, and the world grew so angry.

Then one day the fires came. I was on the outskirts, and my mind is well-protected. But the cancer came for my children. I could not protect them. Some left. Some died. And now I am alone.

But I still remember, in my walls, the children used to sing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tall, did you just magically skip the the last two paragraphs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What gets it for me is the picture of the people sitting at the desks. Sort of removes a sense of danger from the building, since someone obviously was with them to take the pictures and was able to exit safely to upload the image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I think there is a lack of formal tone in the article that could help it seem more like a report and less like a warning about the big scary school, for example;

'Others were as old and wrecked as the main structure.'

Is rather informal phrasing (not to mention it is something of a sentence fragment), perhaps something like;

'Other sections found showed similar wear to initially surveyed sections.'

Phrases in that 'tone' show up throughout the article and I think detract from the feel of the report.

In addition, I am also kind of tired of the 'it's bigger on the inside' cliche that seems to be common on most building based SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I might suggest, the description seems very anecdotal. Cutting down the description section to the bare, clinical essentials could leave room for some very creepy interviews, exploration logs, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rated up in the hope of future amazingness. To me it sounded like the kind of place school feels like as a child, abnormally large and boring.

Again, unsure of a hook, but it seems like a place that stores the things from al those old and dusty storerooms every school has tucked away somewhere in their floorplan, where unused books are kept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting idea, but… I don't. There seems to be some hook here that isn't actually mentioned, or alluded to, that could turn this into a GREAT SCP, but I have no idea what it is. IT actually seems kind of , well, dull, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was definitely going to comment on the second aspect. I'm not sure how exactly this differ from something SCP-184 (besides that SCP's creation getting more outlandish than this) might generate, and the student thing is, IMO, average at best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, and I'm going to upvote it on the basis of the article itself. But two things stick out in my mind:

It says that graffiti fades away quickly but the pictures have it all over the place.

The other isn't a quibble with this SCP /specifically/ but I've about reached my tolerance limit on places and buildings that are bigger and more complicated on the inside than the outside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2018 EDIT:

The first two images are borrowed from:

Original Source: https://www.flickr.com/photos/42311564@N00/sets/72157607627574278/
Creator: Sebastian Niedlich

They are used here with the generous permission of Mr. Niedlich. Their use here does not constitute nor imply that Mr. Niedlich has released them under Creative Commons.

I've incorporated a couple of Gears' more minor suggestions, but largely left the article unchanged, until I get a better feel for how people think of the article as a whole.
